question:
5. Let \( k \) be a positive integer, and \( n \) a positive integer greater than 2 . Define
\[
f_{1}(n)=n, f_{2}(n)=n^{f_{1}(n)}, \ldots, f_{j+1}(n)=n^{f_{j}(n)}, \text { etc. }
\]

Prove either part of the inequality
\[
f_{k}(n)<n!!!\cdots!<f_{k+1}(n)
\]
where the middle term has \( k \) factorial symbols.
solution:
Solution. Define \( g_{1}(n)=n!, g_{k+1}(n)=\left[g_{k}(n)\right]! \). Then the required inequalities are
\[
f_{k}(n)<g_{k}(n)<f_{k+1}(n), \quad n>2 .
\]

Proof of the lower inequality. If \( t \geq 2 n^{2} \), then
\[
t!>\left(n^{2}\right)^{\prime-n^{2}}=n^{\prime} \cdot n^{\prime-2 n^{2}} \geq n^{\prime}
\]

Also, if \( n=3 \) or \( 4, k \geq 2 \), then
\[
g_{k}(n) \geq g_{2}(3)=6!=720>32 \geq 2 n^{2}
\]
and if \( n \geq 5, k \geq 1 \),
\[
g_{k}(n) \geq n!\geq n(n-1)(n-2)>2 n^{2} .
\]

Now obviously \( f_{1}(n)<g_{1}(n) \) for any integer \( n \geq 3 \) and \( f_{2}(3)<f_{2}(4)= \) \( 256<720=g_{2}(3)<g_{2}(4) \). We proceed by induction on \( n \). Assume \( f_{k}(n) \) \( <g_{k}(n) \) where \( n \geq 3 \), and \( k \geq 2 \) if \( n=3 \) or 4 . Then \( g_{k}(n)>2 n^{2} \), so by (1)
\[
f_{k+1}(n)=n^{f_{k}(n)}<n^{g k(n)}<\left[g_{k}(n)\right]!=g_{k+1}(n) .
\]

This completes the inductive proof of the lower inequality.
Proof of the upper inequality. We note first that \( g_{k+1}(n)=g_{k}(n!) \) and put \( g_{0}(n)=n \).

We shall prove by induction on \( k \) that
\[
g_{0}(n) g_{1}(n) g_{2}(n) \cdots g_{k}(n)<n^{g_{0}(n) g_{1}(n) g_{2}(n) \cdots g_{k-1}(n)}
\]
for \( k \geq 1 \) and \( n>2 \).
First, we consider \( k=1 \) and \( n>2 \). We have
\[
n \cdot n!=n \cdot n(n-1) \cdots 3 \cdot 2<n \cdot n \cdot n \cdots n \cdot n=n^{n},
\]
i.e.,
\[
g_{0}(n) g_{1}(n)<n^{g 0(n)} .
\]

Now assume that (2) holds for a fixed \( k \) and all \( n>2 \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
g_{0}(n) g_{1}(n) g_{2}(n) \cdots g_{k+1}(n) & =g_{0}(n) g_{0}(n!) g_{1}(n!) \cdots g_{k}(n!) \\
& <n(n!)^{g_{0}(n!) \cdots g_{k-1}(n!)} \\
& \leq n^{g_{1}(n) \cdots g_{k}(n)} \cdot(n!)^{g_{1}(n) \cdots g_{k}(n)} \\
& =(n n!)^{g_{1}(n) \cdots g_{k}(n)} \\
& <\left(n^{g_{0}(n) g_{1}(n) \cdots g_{k}(n)}\right. \\
& =n^{g_{0}(n) g_{1}(n) \cdots g_{k}(n)} .
\end{aligned}
\]

This completes the proof of (2) by induction on \( k \).
Now we shall prove
\[
g_{0}(n) g_{1}(n) \cdots g_{k}(n)<f_{k+1}(n)
\]
for \( k \geq 1 \) and \( n \geq 2 \). (Obviously there is equality for \( k=0 \) ). For \( k=1 \), this is \( n \cdot n!<n^{n} \), which we have already established. If we assume (3) for a fixed \( k \) and use (2) we obtain
\[
g_{0}(n) g_{1}(n) \cdots g_{k+1}(n)<n^{g_{0}(n) g_{1}(n) \cdots g_{k}(n)}<n^{f_{k+1}(n)}=f_{k+2}(n),
\]
which is (3) for \( k+1 \). Hence (3) for all \( k \) follows by induction. Obviously (3) implies the required upper inequality
\[
g_{k}(n)<f_{k+1}(n) .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
5. Let \( ladderindex \) be a positive integer, and \( indexinteger \) a positive integer greater than 2. Define
\[
primarytower(indexinteger)=indexinteger,\; secondarytower(indexinteger)=indexinteger^{primarytower(indexinteger)},\; \ldots,\; incrementtower(indexinteger)=indexinteger^{generaltower(indexinteger)}, \text{ etc. }
\]

Prove either part of the inequality
\[
leveltower(indexinteger)<indexinteger!!!\cdots!<forthcomingtower(indexinteger)
\]
where the middle term has \( ladderindex \) factorial symbols.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Define \( firstfactor(indexinteger)=indexinteger!,\; nextfactor(indexinteger)=\left[levelfactor(indexinteger)\right]! \). Then the required inequalities are
\[
leveltower(indexinteger)<levelfactor(indexinteger)<forthcomingtower(indexinteger), \quad indexinteger>2.
\]

Proof of the lower inequality. If \( boundinteger \ge 2\,indexinteger^{2} \), then
\[
boundinteger!>\left(indexinteger^{2}\right)^{\prime-indexinteger^{2}}=indexinteger^{\prime}\cdot indexinteger^{\prime-2\,indexinteger^{2}} \ge indexinteger^{\prime}.
\]

Also, if \( indexinteger=3 \) or \( 4, ladderindex \ge 2 \), then
\[
levelfactor(indexinteger) \ge secondfactor(3)=6!=720>32 \ge 2\,indexinteger^{2}
\]
and if \( indexinteger \ge 5, ladderindex \ge 1 \),
\[
levelfactor(indexinteger) \ge indexinteger!\ge indexinteger(indexinteger-1)(indexinteger-2)>2\,indexinteger^{2}.
\]

Obviously \( primarytower(indexinteger)<firstfactor(indexinteger) \) for any integer \( indexinteger \ge 3 \) and \( secondarytower(3)<secondarytower(4)=256<720=secondfactor(3)<secondfactor(4) \). Proceed by induction on \( indexinteger \). Assume \( leveltower(indexinteger)<levelfactor(indexinteger) \) where \( indexinteger \ge 3 \) and \( ladderindex \ge 2 \) if \( indexinteger=3 \) or 4. Then \( levelfactor(indexinteger)>2\,indexinteger^{2} \), so by (1)
\[
forthcomingtower(indexinteger)=indexinteger^{leveltower(indexinteger)}<indexinteger^{levelfactor(indexinteger)}<\left[levelfactor(indexinteger)\right]!=nextfactor(indexinteger).
\]
This completes the inductive proof of the lower inequality.

Proof of the upper inequality. First note that \( nextfactor(indexinteger)=levelfactor(indexinteger!) \) and set \( zerofactor(indexinteger)=indexinteger \).

We prove by induction on \( ladderindex \) that
\[
zerofactor(indexinteger)\,firstfactor(indexinteger)\,secondfactor(indexinteger)\cdots levelfactor(indexinteger)<indexinteger^{zerofactor(indexinteger)\,firstfactor(indexinteger)\,secondfactor(indexinteger)\cdots previousfactor(indexinteger)}
\]
for \( ladderindex \ge 1 \) and \( indexinteger>2 \).

For \( ladderindex=1 \) and \( indexinteger>2 \),
\[
indexinteger\cdot indexinteger!=indexinteger\cdot indexinteger(indexinteger-1)\cdots 3\cdot 2<indexinteger\cdot indexinteger\cdot indexinteger\cdots indexinteger=indexinteger^{indexinteger},
\]
that is,
\[
zerofactor(indexinteger)\,firstfactor(indexinteger)<indexinteger^{zerofactor(indexinteger)}.
\]

Assume (2) holds for some fixed \( ladderindex \) and all \( indexinteger>2 \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
zerofactor(indexinteger)\,firstfactor(indexinteger)\,secondfactor(indexinteger)\cdots nextfactor(indexinteger)
&=zerofactor(indexinteger)\,zerofactor(indexinteger!)\,firstfactor(indexinteger!)\cdots levelfactor(indexinteger!)\\
&<indexinteger\,(indexinteger!)^{zerofactor(indexinteger!)\cdots previousfactor(indexinteger!)}\\
&\le indexinteger^{firstfactor(indexinteger)\cdots levelfactor(indexinteger)}\,(indexinteger!)^{firstfactor(indexinteger)\cdots levelfactor(indexinteger)}\\
&=(indexinteger\,indexinteger!)^{firstfactor(indexinteger)\cdots levelfactor(indexinteger)}\\
&<indexinteger^{zerofactor(indexinteger)\,firstfactor(indexinteger)\cdots levelfactor(indexinteger)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus (2) holds for \( ladderindex+1 \) and hence for all \( ladderindex \).

Now we prove
\[
zerofactor(indexinteger)\,firstfactor(indexinteger)\cdots levelfactor(indexinteger)<forthcomingtower(indexinteger)
\]
for \( ladderindex \ge 1 \) and \( indexinteger \ge 2 \). Equality is clear for \( ladderindex=0 \). For \( ladderindex=1 \), the statement reduces to \( indexinteger\cdot indexinteger!<indexinteger^{indexinteger} \), already shown. Assuming (3) for some \( ladderindex \) and using (2), we get
\[
zerofactor(indexinteger)\,firstfactor(indexinteger)\cdots nextfactor(indexinteger)<indexinteger^{zerofactor(indexinteger)\,firstfactor(indexinteger)\cdots levelfactor(indexinteger)}<indexinteger^{forthcomingtower(indexinteger)}=subsequenttower(indexinteger),
\]
which is (3) for \( ladderindex+1 \). Hence (3) holds for all \( ladderindex \), and in particular it implies
\[
levelfactor(indexinteger)<forthcomingtower(indexinteger).
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. Let \( bluefinch \) be a positive integer, and \( sunflower \) a positive integer greater than 2 . Define
\[
blueberry(sunflower)=sunflower, buttermilk(sunflower)=sunflower^{blueberry(sunflower)}, \ldots, dragonfly(sunflower)=sunflower^{stonewall(sunflower)}, \text { etc. }
\]

Prove either part of the inequality
\[
hummingjay(sunflower)<sunflower!!!\cdots!<marshmallow(sunflower)
\]
where the middle term has \( bluefinch \) factorial symbols.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Define \( alligator(sunflower)=sunflower!, mastermind(sunflower)=\left[thunderbolt(sunflower)\right]! \). Then the required inequalities are
\[
hummingjay(sunflower)<thunderbolt(sunflower)<marshmallow(sunflower), \quad sunflower>2 .
\]

Proof of the lower inequality. If \( pinecones \geq 2 sunflower^{2} \), then
\[
pinecones!>\left(sunflower^{2}\right)^{\prime-sunflower^{2}}=sunflower^{\prime} \cdot sunflower^{\prime-2 sunflower^{2}} \geq sunflower^{\prime}
\]

Also, if \( sunflower=3 \) or \( 4, bluefinch \geq 2 \), then
\[
thunderbolt(sunflower) \geq cottonwood(3)=6!=720>32 \geq 2 sunflower^{2}
\]
and if \( sunflower \geq 5, bluefinch \geq 1 \),
\[
thunderbolt(sunflower) \geq sunflower!\geq sunflower(sunflower-1)(sunflower-2)>2 sunflower^{2} .
\]

Now obviously \( blueberry(sunflower)<alligator(sunflower) \) for any integer \( sunflower \geq 3 \) and \( buttermilk(3)<buttermilk(4)= \) \( 256<720=cottonwood(3)<cottonwood(4) \). We proceed by induction on \( sunflower \). Assume \( hummingjay(sunflower) \)
\( <thunderbolt(sunflower) \) where \( sunflower \geq 3 \), and \( bluefinch \geq 2 \) if \( sunflower=3 \) or 4 . Then \( thunderbolt(sunflower)>2 sunflower^{2} \), so by (1)
\[
marshmallow(sunflower)=sunflower^{hummingjay(sunflower)}<sunflower^{thunderbolt(sunflower)}<\left[thunderbolt(sunflower)\right]!=mastermind(sunflower) .
\]

This completes the inductive proof of the lower inequality.
Proof of the upper inequality. We note first that \( mastermind(sunflower)=thunderbolt(sunflower!) \) and put \( rattlesnake(sunflower)=sunflower \).

We shall prove by induction on \( bluefinch \) that
\[
rattlesnake(sunflower) alligator(sunflower) cottonwood(sunflower) \cdots thunderbolt(sunflower)<sunflower^{rattlesnake(sunflower) alligator(sunflower) cottonwood(sunflower) \cdots switchblade(sunflower)}
\]
for \( bluefinch \geq 1 \) and \( sunflower>2 \).
First, we consider \( bluefinch=1 \) and \( sunflower>2 \). We have
\[
sunflower \cdot sunflower!=sunflower \cdot sunflower(sunflower-1) \cdots 3 \cdot 2<sunflower \cdot sunflower \cdot sunflower \cdots sunflower \cdot sunflower=sunflower^{sunflower},
\]
i.e.,
\[
rattlesnake(sunflower) alligator(sunflower)<sunflower^{rattlesnake(sunflower)} .
\]

Now assume that (2) holds for a fixed \( bluefinch \) and all \( sunflower>2 \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
rattlesnake(sunflower) alligator(sunflower) cottonwood(sunflower) \cdots mastermind(sunflower) & =rattlesnake(sunflower) rattlesnake(sunflower!) alligator(sunflower!) \cdots thunderbolt(sunflower!) \
& <sunflower(sunflower!)^{rattlesnake(sunflower!) \cdots switchblade(sunflower!)} \\
& \leq sunflower^{alligator(sunflower) \cdots thunderbolt(sunflower)} \cdot(sunflower!)^{alligator(sunflower) \cdots thunderbolt(sunflower)} \\
& =(sunflower \ sunflower!)^{alligator(sunflower) \cdots thunderbolt(sunflower)} \\
& <\left(sunflower^{rattlesnake(sunflower) alligator(sunflower) \cdots thunderbolt(sunflower)}\right. \\
& =sunflower^{rattlesnake(sunflower) alligator(sunflower) \cdots thunderbolt(sunflower)} .
\end{aligned}
\]

This completes the proof of (2) by induction on \( bluefinch \).
Now we shall prove
\[
rattlesnake(sunflower) alligator(sunflower) \cdots thunderbolt(sunflower)<marshmallow(sunflower)
\]
for \( bluefinch \geq 1 \) and \( sunflower \geq 2 \). (Obviously there is equality for \( bluefinch=0 \) ). For \( bluefinch=1 \), this is \( sunflower \cdot sunflower!<sunflower^{sunflower} \), which we have already established. If we assume (3) for a fixed \( bluefinch \) and use (2) we obtain
\[
rattlesnake(sunflower) alligator(sunflower) \cdots mastermind(sunflower)<sunflower^{rattlesnake(sunflower) alligator(sunflower) \cdots thunderbolt(sunflower)}<sunflower^{marshmallow(sunflower)}=cheesecake(sunflower),
\]
which is (3) for \( bluefinch+1 \). Hence (3) for all \( bluefinch \) follows by induction. Obviously (3) implies the required upper inequality
\[
thunderbolt(sunflower)<marshmallow(sunflower) .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
5. Let \( minimums \) be a positive integer, and \( negativefraction \) a positive integer greater than 2 . Define
\[
flatfuncone(negativefraction)=negativefraction, flatfunctwo(negativefraction)=negativefraction^{flatfuncone(negativefraction)}, \ldots, flatfuncnext(negativefraction)=negativefraction^{flatfuncend(negativefraction)}, \text { etc. }
\]

Prove either part of the inequality
\[
flatfunck(negativefraction)<negativefraction!!!\cdots!<flatfuncknext(negativefraction)
\]
where the middle term has \( minimums \) factorial symbols.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Define \( divideone(negativefraction)=negativefraction!, divideknext(negativefraction)=\left[dividekay(negativefraction)\right]! \). Then the required inequalities are
\[
flatfunck(negativefraction)<dividekay(negativefraction)<flatfuncknext(negativefraction), \quad negativefraction>2 .
\]

Proof of the lower inequality. If \( smallnumber \geq 2 negativefraction^{2} \), then
\[
smallnumber!>\left(negativefraction^{2}\right)^{\prime-negativefraction^{2}}=negativefraction^{\prime} \cdot negativefraction^{\prime-2 negativefraction^{2}} \geq negativefraction^{\prime}
\]

Also, if \( negativefraction=3 \) or \( 4, minimums \geq 2 \), then
\[
dividekay(negativefraction) \geq divideone(3)=6!=720>32 \geq 2 negativefraction^{2}
\]
and if \( negativefraction \geq 5, minimums \geq 1 \),
\[
dividekay(negativefraction) \geq negativefraction!\geq negativefraction(negativefraction-1)(negativefraction-2)>2 negativefraction^{2} .
\]

Now obviously \( flatfuncone(negativefraction)<divideone(negativefraction) \) for any integer \( negativefraction \geq 3 \) and \( flatfunctwo(3)<flatfunctwo(4)=256<720=dividetwo(3)<dividetwo(4) \). We proceed by induction on \( negativefraction \). Assume \( flatfunck(negativefraction) <dividekay(negativefraction) \) where \( negativefraction \geq 3 \), and \( minimums \geq 2 \) if \( negativefraction=3 \) or 4 . Then \( dividekay(negativefraction)>2 negativefraction^{2} \), so by (1)
\[
flatfuncknext(negativefraction)=negativefraction^{flatfunck(negativefraction)}<negativefraction^{dividekay(negativefraction)}<\left[dividekay(negativefraction)\right]!=divideknext(negativefraction) .
\]

This completes the inductive proof of the lower inequality.
Proof of the upper inequality. We note first that \( divideknext(negativefraction)=dividekay(negativefraction!) \) and put \( dividezero(negativefraction)=negativefraction \).

We shall prove by induction on \( minimums \) that
\[
dividezero(negativefraction) divideone(negativefraction) dividetwo(negativefraction) \cdots dividekay(negativefraction)<negativefraction^{dividezero(negativefraction) divideone(negativefraction) dividetwo(negativefraction) \cdots dividekprev(negativefraction)}
\]
for \( minimums \geq 1 \) and \( negativefraction>2 \).
First, we consider \( minimums=1 \) and \( negativefraction>2 \). We have
\[
negativefraction \cdot negativefraction!=negativefraction \cdot negativefraction(negativefraction-1) \cdots 3 \cdot 2<negativefraction \cdot negativefraction \cdot negativefraction \cdots negativefraction \cdot negativefraction=negativefraction^{negativefraction},
\]
i.e.,
\[
dividezero(negativefraction) divideone(negativefraction)<negativefraction^{dividezero(negativefraction)} .
\]

Now assume that (2) holds for a fixed \( minimums \) and all \( negativefraction>2 \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
dividezero(negativefraction) divideone(negativefraction) dividetwo(negativefraction) \cdots divideknext(negativefraction) & =dividezero(negativefraction) dividezero(negativefraction!) divideone(negativefraction!) \cdots dividekay(negativefraction!) \
& <negativefraction(negativefraction!)^{dividezero(negativefraction!) \cdots dividekprev(negativefraction!)} \
& \leq negativefraction^{divideone(negativefraction) \cdots dividekay(negativefraction)} \cdot(negativefraction!)^{divideone(negativefraction) \cdots dividekay(negativefraction)} \
& =(negativefraction negativefraction!)^{divideone(negativefraction) \cdots dividekay(negativefraction)} \
& <\left(negativefraction^{dividezero(negativefraction) divideone(negativefraction) \cdots dividekay(negativefraction)}\right. \
& =negativefraction^{dividezero(negativefraction) divideone(negativefraction) \cdots dividekay(negativefraction)} .
\end{aligned}
\]

This completes the proof of (2) by induction on \( minimums \).
Now we shall prove
\[
dividezero(negativefraction) divideone(negativefraction) \cdots dividekay(negativefraction)<flatfuncknext(negativefraction)
\]
for \( minimums \geq 1 \) and \( negativefraction \geq 2 \). (Obviously there is equality for \( minimums=0 \) ). For \( minimums=1 \), this is \( negativefraction \cdot negativefraction!<negativefraction^{negativefraction} \), which we have already established. If we assume (3) for a fixed \( minimums \) and use (2) we obtain
\[
dividezero(negativefraction) divideone(negativefraction) \cdots divideknext(negativefraction)<negativefraction^{dividezero(negativefraction) divideone(negativefraction) \cdots dividekay(negativefraction)}<negativefraction^{flatfuncknext(negativefraction)}=flatfuncktwo(negativefraction),
\]
which is (3) for \( minimums+1 \). Hence (3) for all \( minimums \) follows by induction. Obviously (3) implies the required upper inequality
\[
dividekay(negativefraction)<flatfuncknext(negativefraction) .
\]

garbled_string.question:
5. Let \( asduvtrm \) be a positive integer, and \( qzxwvtnp \) a positive integer greater than 2 . Define
\[
djwknrla(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp, mlsgtqpo(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{djwknrla(qzxwvtnp)}, \ldots, onzptria(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{kzvabmxe(qzxwvtnp)}, \text { etc. }
\]

Prove either part of the inequality
\[
vgbekzny(qzxwvtnp)<qzxwvtnp!!!\cdots!<ewbnlmso(qzxwvtnp)
\]
where the middle term has \( asduvtrm \) factorial symbols.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Define \( xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp!, bhmcieaw(qzxwvtnp)=\left[cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)\right]! \). Then the required inequalities are
\[
vgbekzny(qzxwvtnp)<cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)<ewbnlmso(qzxwvtnp), \quad qzxwvtnp>2 .
\]

Proof of the lower inequality. If \( zhykplom \geq 2 qzxwvtnp^{2} \), then
\[
zhykplom!>\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}\right)^{\prime-qzxwvtnp^{2}}=qzxwvtnp^{\prime} \cdot qzxwvtnp^{\prime-2 qzxwvtnp^{2}} \geq qzxwvtnp^{\prime}
\]

Also, if \( qzxwvtnp=3 \) or \( 4, asduvtrm \geq 2 \), then
\[
cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp) \geq utdzheqa(3)=6!=720>32 \geq 2 qzxwvtnp^{2}
\]
and if \( qzxwvtnp \geq 5, asduvtrm \geq 1 \),
\[
cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp) \geq qzxwvtnp!\geq qzxwvtnp(qzxwvtnp-1)(qzxwvtnp-2)>2 qzxwvtnp^{2} .
\]

Now obviously \( djwknrla(qzxwvtnp)<xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) \) for any integer \( qzxwvtnp \geq 3 \) and \( mlsgtqpo(3)<mlsgtqpo(4)=256<720=utdzheqa(3)<utdzheqa(4) \). We proceed by induction on \( qzxwvtnp \). Assume \( vgbekzny(qzxwvtnp)<cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp) \) where \( qzxwvtnp \geq 3 \), and \( asduvtrm \geq 2 \) if \( qzxwvtnp=3 \) or 4 . Then \( cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)>2 qzxwvtnp^{2} \), so by (1)
\[
ewbnlmso(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{vgbekzny(qzxwvtnp)}<qzxwvtnp^{cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)}<\left[cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)\right]!=bhmcieaw(qzxwvtnp) .
\]

This completes the inductive proof of the lower inequality.

Proof of the upper inequality. We note first that \( bhmcieaw(qzxwvtnp)=cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp!) \) and put \( lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp \).

We shall prove by induction on \( asduvtrm \) that
\[
lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp) xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) utdzheqa(qzxwvtnp) \cdots cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)<qzxwvtnp^{lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp) xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) utdzheqa(qzxwvtnp) \cdots rtyufbsa(qzxwvtnp)}
\]
for \( asduvtrm \geq 1 \) and \( qzxwvtnp>2 \).

First, we consider \( asduvtrm=1 \) and \( qzxwvtnp>2 \). We have
\[
qzxwvtnp \cdot qzxwvtnp!=qzxwvtnp \cdot qzxwvtnp(qzxwvtnp-1) \cdots 3 \cdot 2<qzxwvtnp \cdot qzxwvtnp \cdot qzxwvtnp \cdots qzxwvtnp \cdot qzxwvtnp=qzxwvtnp^{qzxwvtnp},
\]
i.e.,
\[
lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp) xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp)<qzxwvtnp^{lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp)} .
\]

Now assume that (2) holds for a fixed \( asduvtrm \) and all \( qzxwvtnp>2 \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp) xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) utdzheqa(qzxwvtnp) \cdots bhmcieaw(qzxwvtnp) & =lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp) lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp!) xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp!) \cdots cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp!) \\
& <qzxwvtnp(qzxwvtnp!)^{lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp!) \cdots rtyufbsa(qzxwvtnp!)} \\
& \leq qzxwvtnp^{xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) \cdots cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)} \cdot(qzxwvtnp!)^{xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) \cdots cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)} \\
& =(qzxwvtnp \, qzxwvtnp!)^{xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) \cdots cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)} \\
& <\left(qzxwvtnp^{lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp) xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) \cdots cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)}\right. \\
& =qzxwvtnp^{lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp) xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) \cdots cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)} .
\end{aligned}
\]

This completes the proof of (2) by induction on \( asduvtrm \).

Now we shall prove
\[
lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp) xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) \cdots cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)<ewbnlmso(qzxwvtnp)
\]
for \( asduvtrm \geq 1 \) and \( qzxwvtnp \geq 2 \). (Obviously there is equality for \( asduvtrm=0 \) ). For \( asduvtrm=1 \), this is \( qzxwvtnp \cdot qzxwvtnp!<qzxwvtnp^{qzxwvtnp} \), which we have already established. If we assume (3) for a fixed \( asduvtrm \) and use (2) we obtain
\[
lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp) xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) \cdots bhmcieaw(qzxwvtnp)<qzxwvtnp^{lyqsnfte(qzxwvtnp) xkprmwdj(qzxwvtnp) \cdots cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)}<qzxwvtnp^{ewbnlmso(qzxwvtnp)}=pqudchxr(qzxwvtnp),
\]
which is (3) for \( asduvtrm+1 \). Hence (3) for all \( asduvtrm \) follows by induction. Obviously (3) implies the required upper inequality
\[
cvhplogi(qzxwvtnp)<ewbnlmso(qzxwvtnp) .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let k be a positive integer and let n be an integer strictly larger than 3.  Define two sequences by

f_1(n)=n,  f_{j+1}(n)=n^{f_{j}(n)} (j \geq  1),

h_1(n)=n!,  h_{j+1}(n)=\bigl(h_{j}(n)\bigr)! (j \geq  1).

Thus h_k(n)=n!!!\cdots ! with exactly k factorial signs.  Prove that for every n>3 and every positive integer k one has the strict double inequality

                f_k(n) < h_k(n) < f_{k+1}(n).
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout the proof we fix an integer n>3 and suppress the argument n, writing simply f_j and h_j (j \geq  1).
We must establish for every k \geq  1
(I)  f_k < h_k and (II)  h_k < f_{k+1}.
The two inequalities are treated separately.

-------------------------------------------------
1.  Two auxiliary estimates
-------------------------------------------------
Lemma 1.  (Factorials eventually dominate exponentials)
For every integer n \geq  3 and every integer t \geq  2n^2 one has t! > n^t.

Proof.  Write t! as the product of the first n^2 factors and the remaining t-n^2 factors:

t! = (1\cdots n^2) \cdot  (n^2+1)\cdots t \geq  (n^2)! \cdot  (n^2)^{t-n^2}.             (1)

A standard consequence of Stirling's formula is m! > (m/e)^m for m \geq  1; taking m=n^2 \geq  9 gives

        (n^2)! > (n^2/e)^{n^2} \geq  n^{n^2}                        (2)
(the last inequality uses n^2/e \geq  n for n \geq  3).  Combining (1) and (2) we obtain

t! > n^{n^2} \cdot  (n^2)^{t-n^2} = n^{n^2}\cdot n^{2(t-n^2)} = n^{2t-n^2}.

Because t \geq  2n^2, the exponent 2t-n^2 is at least t, so t! > n^t.
\blacksquare 

Lemma 2.  (A crude upper bound for a factorial)
For every integer m \geq  2 one has m! < m^{m}.
Indeed, each factor in m! is at most m.
\blacksquare 

-------------------------------------------------
2.  The lower inequality  f_k < h_k
-------------------------------------------------
Base step k=1.  Because n>3, f_1 = n < n! = h_1.

Inductive step.  Assume f_k < h_k for some k \geq  1.

*  If h_k \geq  2n^2, Lemma 1 with t=h_k gives
  h_{k+1}=h_k! > n^{h_k} > n^{f_k}=f_{k+1},
so f_{k+1} < h_{k+1}.

*  If h_k < 2n^2 we must have (n,k)=(4,1):
    - for n \geq  5 we have n! = h_1 \geq  n(n-1)(n-2) > 2n^2;
    - for n = 4, h_1 = 24 < 2\cdot 4^2 = 32, while h_2 = 24! >> 2\cdot 4^2.
  A direct check shows f_2(4)=4^4=256 < 24! = h_2(4).

Hence in every case f_{k+1} < h_{k+1}, completing the induction and proving f_k < h_k for all k.
\blacksquare 

-------------------------------------------------
3.  A growth-comparison lemma
-------------------------------------------------
Lemma 3.  Let n \geq  4.  For every real x \geq  n one has x\cdot log_n x < n^{x}.

Proof.  Define g(x) = n^{x} - x\cdot log_n x.  Because g(n)=n^{n}-n>0 it suffices to show g'(x) > 0 for x \geq  n.

 g'(x) = n^{x} ln n - (ln x + 1)/ln n.

For x \geq  n \geq  4 we have ln x + 1 \leq  x \leq  n^{x} ln^2n, so the right-hand term is dominated by n^{x} ln n and g'(x) is positive.  Thus g(x) increases and stays positive for x \geq  n, proving the claim.
\blacksquare 

-------------------------------------------------
4.  The upper inequality  h_k < f_{k+1}
-------------------------------------------------
We again argue by induction on k.

Base step k=1.
  h_1 = n! < n^{n} = f_2 (by Lemma 2).

Inductive step.  Assume h_k < f_{k+1}.  Set A = h_k.  Then

(1) h_{k+1} = A! < A^{A} (by Lemma 2).

(2) Because x \mapsto  x^{x} is increasing for x \geq  e and A < f_{k+1}, we have
  A^{A} < f_{k+1}^{f_{k+1}}.

(3) Rewrite the right-hand side with base n:
  f_{k+1}^{f_{k+1}} = (n^{f_{k+1}})^{f_{k+1}} = n^{f_{k+1}\cdot log_n f_{k+1}}.
  By Lemma 3 (with x = f_{k+1} \geq  n) we get
  f_{k+1}\cdot log_n f_{k+1} < n^{f_{k+1}} = f_{k+2}.
  Hence
  n^{f_{k+1}\cdot log_n f_{k+1}} < n^{f_{k+2}} = f_{k+2}.

Chaining (1)-(3) yields
  h_{k+1} < f_{k+2}.
Thus the induction advances and h_k < f_{k+1} holds for all k.
\blacksquare 

-------------------------------------------------
5.  Conclusion
-------------------------------------------------
The two independent inductions give, for every integer n>3 and every k \geq  1,
  f_k(n) < h_k(n) < f_{k+1}(n).
\blacksquare

core_steps: Growth-comparison lemma: for sufficiently large t (≥ C·n^α) one has t! > n^t
Size assurance: prove g_k(n) already exceeds that threshold, via small-n check and/or larger k
Inductive amplification: f_k(n) < g_k(n) ⇒ f_{k+1}(n) < g_{k+1}(n), giving the lower bound
Product-vs-exponential lemma: Π_{i=0}^{k} g_i(n) < n^{Π_{i=0}^{k-1} g_i(n)} proved by induction using g_{k+1}(n)=g_k(n!)
Use the product bound to inductively obtain g_k(n) < f_{k+1}(n), completing the upper bound

slot1.description: multiplicative constant in the threshold where factorial overtakes n^t

slot2.description: power of n used in that threshold

slot3.description: list of small n that require a higher k to clear the threshold

slot4.description: first n for which the direct estimate n! > threshold is used
